schools.summary: The case of a prophet who gives a sign or wonder: 'if there arise among you a prophet, or a dreamer of dreams, and giveth thee a sign or a wonder' — the case is put of a prophet or dreamer who offers a sign or wonder (the if there arise among you a prophet, or a dreamer of dreams, the false prophet or dreamer, the and giveth thee a sign or a wonder, the sign offered as proof, the figure, the case of the false prophet who works a sign to lead astray, the truth that a sign or wonder is not proof of truth if it leads to other gods, the figure of the test of the prophet, the wonder weighed against the word of God, the false prophet who may even give a sign, yet is to be tried by his doctrine, vv.2-3, the warning that miracles alone do not authenticate a teacher, 'though we, or an angel from heaven, preach any other gospel,' Galatians 1:8); the case of a false prophet or dreamer (the test) — the prophet (or dreamer); 'and giveth thee a sign or a wonder' (the sign offered as proof, the wonder weighed by the word, Galatians 1:8, the figure, the test of the prophet by his doctrine, the providence, the case, the sign coming to pass yet enticing to other gods to follow, vv.2) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: If there arise among you a prophet, or a dreamer of dreams, and giveth thee a sign or a wonder
the case of a prophet who gives a sign or wonder
the false prophet who may even give a sign or wonder, yet is to be tried by his doctrine
the wonder weighed against the word of God (Galatians 1:8)

schools.summary: The sign coming to pass, yet the prophet enticing to other gods: 'and the sign or the wonder come to pass, whereof he spake unto thee, saying, Let us go after other gods, which thou hast not known, and let us serve them' — even though the sign came true, if the prophet enticed to other gods, he was not to be heard (the and the sign or the wonder come to pass, whereof he spake unto thee, the sign fulfilled, the saying, Let us go after other gods, which thou hast not known, and let us serve them, the enticement to idolatry, the figure, the sign fulfilled, yet the doctrine false, the enticement to other gods the test, the truth that even a true sign does not justify a call to idolatry, the doctrine condemning the prophet, the figure of the false teacher whose wonder is real but whose message is apostasy, the enticement to other gods the mark of the false prophet, however his sign succeed, the test that weighs the message, not the miracle, vv.3); the sign coming to pass (the wonder real) — the sign (fulfilled); 'Let us go after other gods... and let us serve them' (the enticement to idolatry, the false doctrine, the mark of the false prophet, the figure, the message that condemns the prophet, the providence, the enticement, the LORD proving them to follow, vv.3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the sign or the wonder come to pass, whereof he spake unto thee, saying, Let us go after other gods, which thou hast not known, and let us serve them
the sign coming to pass, yet the prophet enticing to other gods
even a true sign not justifying a call to idolatry, the enticement to other gods the mark of the false prophet
the test that weighs the message, not the miracle

schools.summary: That the false prophet is not to be heard, for the LORD proves the people by him: 'thou shalt not hearken unto the words of that prophet, or that dreamer of dreams: for the LORD your God proveth you, to know whether ye love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul' — the proving — 'thou shalt not hearken unto the words of that prophet, or that dreamer of dreams: for the LORD your God proveth you, to know whether ye love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul': the false prophet, even with his sign, is not to be heard, for God permits the test to prove their love (the witnesses noting the significance — God's permission of the false prophet's sign is a test of the people's love and loyalty, the LORD proves them, to see whether they will cleave to him or be drawn away, the sign permitted, not to deceive, but to try the heart, the lesson — that the love of God is to be supreme, above all signs and wonders, the test whether they love him with all the heart, the heart's loyalty weighed, the false prophet the occasion of proving it, the truth that God tries his people, suffering temptations to arise that their faith and love be tested and shown, the truth that no sign or wonder is to draw the heart from God, the love of God the highest loyalty, 'I will love thee, O LORD, my strength,' Psalm 18:1, the figure — the testing of faith that proves and purifies it, 'the trial of your faith... might be found unto praise,' 1 Peter 1:7, 'God did tempt Abraham,' Genesis 22:1, the truth that God permits trials, even plausible deceptions, to prove the reality of love and faith, the figure of the heart that loves God supremely standing firm against every enticement, the warning that the test reveals whether the love of God is whole, the figure of the proving of love to God, the test that weighs the heart against the sign); the false prophet not to be heard, however his sign succeed (the message false, vv.2) — the refusal (not hearken); 'for the LORD your God proveth you, to know whether ye love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul' (God proving the people by the false prophet, to test whether they love him with all the heart, the trial of their faith, the test of love, 1 Peter 1:7; Genesis 22:1) — the figure (the testing of faith that proves it, the providence, the test, the call to walk after the LORD and cleave to him to follow, vv.4) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Wesley.

debates.question: Why does God permit the false prophet's sign to succeed?

sides.position: To prove the people, to test whether they love him with all their heart - the love of God to be supreme above all signs and wonders, the trial of faith that proves and purifies it (1 Peter 1:7; Genesis 22:1), God permitting trials and even plausible deceptions that the reality of love and loyalty be shown, no wonder to draw the heart from God (Psalm 18:1)

themes: Thou shalt not hearken unto the words of that prophet, or that dreamer of dreams: for the LORD your God proveth you, to know whether ye love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul
that the false prophet is not to be heard, for the LORD proves the people by him (the crux of the testing of faith and the supremacy of love to God over signs)
God's permission of the false prophet's sign a test of love and loyalty, that the people's whole-hearted love of God be tried and shown
the trial of faith that proves and purifies it, no sign to draw the heart from God (Psalm 18:1; 1 Peter 1:7; Genesis 22:1)

schools.summary: The positive charge to walk after, fear, obey, and cleave to the LORD: 'ye shall walk after the LORD your God, and fear him, and keep his commandments, and obey his voice, and ye shall serve him, and cleave unto him' — against the enticement, the people are charged to follow God wholly (the ye shall walk after the LORD your God, and fear him, walking and fearing, the and keep his commandments, and obey his voice, keeping and obeying, the and ye shall serve him, and cleave unto him, serving and cleaving, the figure, the sixfold charge of whole-hearted loyalty to God, against every enticement, the truth that the answer to the false prophet is steadfast cleaving to God, walking, fearing, obeying, serving, the figure of the perseverance of faith, the heart fixed on God, 'cleave unto the LORD with purpose of heart,' Acts 11:23, the comprehensive loyalty that holds fast to God alone, the positive duty that guards against apostasy, the heart cleaving to God); the charge to walk after and fear God (the loyalty, Acts 11:23) — the charge (walk and fear); 'ye shall serve him, and cleave unto him' (the serving and cleaving to God, the perseverance, Acts 11:23, the figure, the heart fixed on God, the providence, the charge, the false prophet to be put to death to follow, vv.5) — Henry, Gill.

themes: Ye shall walk after the LORD your God, and fear him, and keep his commandments, and obey his voice, and ye shall serve him, and cleave unto him
the positive charge to walk after, fear, obey, and cleave to the LORD
the people charged to follow God wholly, walking, fearing, obeying, serving, and cleaving
the steadfast loyalty that answers the false prophet's enticement (Acts 11:23)

schools.summary: The false prophet to be put to death for enticing to apostasy: 'and that prophet, or that dreamer of dreams, shall be put to death; because he hath spoken to turn you away from the LORD your God, which brought you out of the land of Egypt, and redeemed you out of the house of bondage, to thrust thee out of the way which the LORD thy God commanded thee to walk in. So shalt thou put the evil away from the midst of thee' — the false prophet was to die for seeking to turn the people from God (the and that prophet, or that dreamer of dreams, shall be put to death, the death penalty, the because he hath spoken to turn you away from the LORD your God, which brought you out of the land of Egypt, the crime, turning from the Redeemer, the to thrust thee out of the way... So shalt thou put the evil away from the midst of thee, the evil purged, the figure, the false prophet judged for seeking to turn the people from their Redeemer, the truth that enticement to apostasy was a capital crime, the evil to be purged from Israel, the figure of the purging of evil from the church, the corrupter put away, 'put away from among yourselves that wicked person,' 1 Corinthians 5:13, the gravity of turning souls from God, the seducer judged, the evil rooted out, that the people be kept pure); the false prophet put to death for turning the people from God (the crime) — the judgment (put to death); 'So shalt thou put the evil away from the midst of thee' (the evil purged from Israel, the corrupter put away, 1 Corinthians 5:13, the figure, the purging of evil from the church, the providence, the judgment, the secret enticer to follow, vv.6-8) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And that prophet, or that dreamer of dreams, shall be put to death; because he hath spoken to turn you away from the LORD your God, which brought you out of the land of Egypt, and redeemed you out of the house of bondage, to thrust thee out of the way which the LORD thy God commanded thee to walk in. So shalt thou put the evil away from the midst of thee
the false prophet to be put to death for enticing to apostasy
the false prophet judged for seeking to turn the people from their Redeemer, the evil purged from Israel
that the people be kept pure (1 Corinthians 5:13)

schools.summary: The case of the secret enticer, even the nearest and dearest: 'if thy brother, the son of thy mother, or thy son, or thy daughter, or the wife of thy bosom, or thy friend, which is as thine own soul, entice thee secretly, saying, Let us go and serve other gods, which thou hast not known, thou, nor thy fathers' — the nearest tie — 'if thy brother, the son of thy mother, or thy son, or thy daughter, or the wife of thy bosom, or thy friend, which is as thine own soul, entice thee secretly, saying, Let us go and serve other gods': the enticer to idolatry, even if the nearest kin or dearest friend, is not to be spared (the witnesses noting the significance — the love of God must be supreme over the closest natural affections, the case heightened by naming the nearest relations, the brother, child, wife of the bosom, the friend as one's own soul, the temptation made most powerful by the dearness of the tempter, yet even so to be resisted, the lesson — that no tie of nature or affection may be allowed to draw the soul from God, the enticement most dangerous when it comes from the closest love, the secret whisper of the dearest, the duty to refuse even them when they would lead to idolatry, the truth that the love of God comes before father, mother, wife, child, and friend, 'he that loveth father or mother more than me is not worthy of me,' Matthew 10:37, 'if any man come to me, and hate not his father, and mother, and wife,' Luke 14:26, the figure — the supremacy of God over the dearest earthly ties, the test of love that prefers God to all, the truth that the strongest natural bonds must yield to loyalty to God, the wife of the bosom and the friend as one's own soul no exception, the figure of the heart wholly God's, that no love however dear may rival him, the warning that the most intimate enticement to apostasy is the most perilous and must be most resolutely refused, the figure of the supremacy of God over the nearest and dearest natural ties); the enticer named as the nearest relations and dearest friend, the temptation heightened by their dearness (the closest ties, Matthew 10:37) — the case (the nearest kin); 'entice thee secretly, saying, Let us go and serve other gods' (the secret whisper to serve other gods, the love of God to be preferred even to the dearest, the supremacy of God, Luke 14:26) — the figure (the heart wholly God's, no love to rival him, the providence, the case, the enticer not to be spared but exposed to follow, vv.8-9) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Wesley.

debates.question: What does the case of the secret enticer, even the nearest kin, establish?

sides.position: The supremacy of God over the closest natural ties - the love of God must come before the brother, child, wife of the bosom, and dearest friend; no tie of nature or affection may draw the soul from God (Matthew 10:37; Luke 14:26), the heart wholly God's that no love however dear may rival him

themes: If thy brother, the son of thy mother, or thy son, or thy daughter, or the wife of thy bosom, or thy friend, which is as thine own soul, entice thee secretly, saying, Let us go and serve other gods, which thou hast not known, thou, nor thy fathers
the case of the secret enticer, even the nearest and dearest (the crux of the supremacy of God over the closest natural ties)
the love of God supreme over the closest affections, the brother, child, wife of the bosom, and dearest friend no exception when they would lead to idolatry
the love of God before father, mother, wife, and child (Matthew 10:37; Luke 14:26)

schools.summary: The gods of the nations, near or far, to which the enticer might draw: 'namely, of the gods of the people which are round about you, nigh unto thee, or far off from thee, from the one end of the earth even unto the other end of the earth' — the enticement might be to any of the gods of the nations, near or far (the namely, of the gods of the people which are round about you, the gods of the surrounding nations, the nigh unto thee, or far off from thee, from the one end of the earth even unto the other, near or far, the figure, the gods of all the nations, near or far, the comprehensive idolatry forbidden, the truth that no idol of any nation, however near or distant, might be served, the figure of the universal prohibition of idolatry, every false god excluded, the enticement to any of the world's gods to be refused, the comprehensive sweep that leaves no idol allowed, vv.6); the gods of the surrounding nations forbidden (the idolatry) — the gods (near); 'far off from thee, from the one end of the earth even unto the other' (the gods near or far all forbidden, the comprehensive prohibition, the figure, no idol of any nation allowed, the providence, the gods, the enticer not to be spared to follow, vv.8) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Namely, of the gods of the people which are round about you, nigh unto thee, or far off from thee, from the one end of the earth even unto the other end of the earth
the gods of the nations, near or far, to which the enticer might draw
the enticement to any of the world's gods, near or far, to be refused
no idol of any nation allowed

schools.summary: The enticer not to be consented to, pitied, spared, or concealed: 'thou shalt not consent unto him, nor hearken unto him; neither shall thine eye pity him, neither shalt thou spare, neither shalt thou conceal him' — the enticer was to be utterly refused, not consented to, pitied, spared, or hidden (the thou shalt not consent unto him, nor hearken unto him, no consent or hearing, the neither shall thine eye pity him, neither shalt thou spare, no pity or sparing, the neither shalt thou conceal him, no concealing, the figure, the resolute refusal of the enticer, no consent, no pity, no concealment, the truth that loyalty to God overrides every natural feeling, even pity for the dearest, the figure of the firmness against apostasy, the enticer not to be shielded, the duty to expose even the nearest who would lead to idolatry, no concealment, the love of God that suffers no rival, however dear, vv.6); the enticer not to be consented to or heard (the firmness) — the refusal (no consent); 'neither shall thine eye pity him, neither shalt thou spare, neither shalt thou conceal him' (no pity, sparing, or concealing, the loyalty above natural feeling, the figure, the firmness against apostasy, the providence, the refusal, the enticer to be the first to die to follow, vv.9) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Thou shalt not consent unto him, nor hearken unto him; neither shall thine eye pity him, neither shalt thou spare, neither shalt thou conceal him
the enticer not to be consented to, pitied, spared, or concealed
the enticer utterly refused, no consent, pity, sparing, or concealment
loyalty to God overriding every natural feeling

schools.summary: The enticer to be put to death, the witness's hand first: 'but thou shalt surely kill him; thine hand shall be first upon him to put him to death, and afterwards the hand of all the people' — the enticer was to die, the one enticed casting the first stone (the but thou shalt surely kill him, the death penalty, the thine hand shall be first upon him to put him to death, the witness's hand first, the and afterwards the hand of all the people, the whole people joining, the figure, the one enticed to be the first witness against the enticer, the hand first upon him, the truth that loyalty to God must overrule even the closest tie, the enticed himself the executioner, the figure of the zeal that prefers God to the dearest, the hand first upon the seducer, the public judgment of apostasy, the people joining, the gravity of enticement to idolatry, the death of the seducer, vv.6); the enticed casting the first stone (the witness's hand) — the judgment (hand first); 'afterwards the hand of all the people' (the whole people joining in the judgment, the public execution, the figure, the zeal against apostasy, the providence, the judgment, the reason of the death to follow, vv.10) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: But thou shalt surely kill him; thine hand shall be first upon him to put him to death, and afterwards the hand of all the people
the enticer to be put to death, the witness's hand first
the enticer to die, the one enticed casting the first stone, loyalty to God overruling the closest tie
the people joining in the judgment

schools.summary: The enticer stoned for seeking to thrust them from God: 'and thou shalt stone him with stones, that he die; because he hath sought to thrust thee away from the LORD thy God, which brought thee out of the land of Egypt, from the house of bondage' — the enticer was to be stoned, because he sought to turn them from their Redeemer (the and thou shalt stone him with stones, that he die, the stoning, the because he hath sought to thrust thee away from the LORD thy God, the reason, turning from God, the which brought thee out of the land of Egypt, from the house of bondage, the Redeemer turned from, the figure, the enticer stoned for the crime of seeking to turn the soul from its Redeemer, the truth that to draw one from God who redeemed them is the gravest of crimes, the figure of the gravity of apostasy, the seducer judged for assaulting loyalty to the Redeemer, the death of the enticer the measure of the crime of turning from God, the Redeemer's claim on the people the ground of the judgment, vv.5); the enticer stoned to death (the judgment) — the stoning (that he die); 'because he hath sought to thrust thee away from the LORD thy God' (the crime of turning from the Redeemer, the gravity of apostasy, the figure, the seducer judged for assaulting loyalty, the providence, the stoning, the fear it would bring to follow, vv.11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt stone him with stones, that he die; because he hath sought to thrust thee away from the LORD thy God, which brought thee out of the land of Egypt, from the house of bondage
the enticer stoned for seeking to thrust them from God
the enticer judged for seeking to turn the soul from its Redeemer, the gravest of crimes
the death the measure of it

schools.summary: That all Israel would hear and fear and do no more such wickedness: 'and all Israel shall hear, and fear, and shall do no more any such wickedness as this is among you' — the public judgment would make all Israel fear and shun such wickedness (the and all Israel shall hear, the report spread, the and fear, the fear produced, the and shall do no more any such wickedness as this is among you, the deterrence, the figure, the public judgment a warning to all, that they fear and shun apostasy, the truth that the judgment of the seducer deters others from the same sin, the figure of the deterrent effect of justice, the people warned by the example, 'those that sin rebuke before all, that others also may fear,' 1 Timothy 5:20, the fear that guards against apostasy, the example a restraint, the judgment that keeps Israel from such wickedness); the fear produced by the judgment (the deterrence, 1 Timothy 5:20) — the fear (all Israel); 'shall do no more any such wickedness as this is among you' (the people kept from such sin, the restraint, 1 Timothy 5:20, the figure, the deterrent effect of justice, the providence, the deterrence, the apostate city to follow, vv.12-18) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And all Israel shall hear, and fear, and shall do no more any such wickedness as this is among you
that all Israel would hear and fear and do no more such wickedness
the public judgment of the seducer making all Israel fear and shun apostasy
the example a restraint that keeps the people from such wickedness (1 Timothy 5:20)

schools.summary: The case of a city reported to have fallen into idolatry: 'if thou shalt hear say in one of thy cities, which the LORD thy God hath given thee to dwell there, saying' — the case is put of a report that a whole city had fallen into idolatry (the if thou shalt hear say in one of thy cities, the report of a city, the which the LORD thy God hath given thee to dwell there, the city of Israel, the saying, the report introduced, the figure, the case of a whole city drawn into apostasy, the gravest spread of idolatry, the truth that even a city given by God might fall, and must be dealt with, the figure of the corruption that may spread from individuals to a whole community, the case enlarged from the single enticer to the apostate city, vv.6, 13, the gravity of corporate apostasy, the city to be tried and judged); the report of a city fallen into idolatry (the case) — the report (a city); 'which the LORD thy God hath given thee to dwell there' (the city of Israel fallen, the corporate apostasy, the figure, the corruption spread to a community, the providence, the case, the men of Belial drawing the city away to follow, vv.13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: If thou shalt hear say in one of thy cities, which the LORD thy God hath given thee to dwell there, saying
the case of a city reported to have fallen into idolatry
a whole city drawn into idolatry, the corruption spread from individuals to a community
the gravest spread of apostasy to be tried and judged

schools.summary: Worthless men drawing the city to serve other gods: 'certain men, the children of Belial, are gone out from among you, and have withdrawn the inhabitants of their city, saying, Let us go and serve other gods, which ye have not known' — worthless men had drawn the city into idolatry (the certain men, the children of Belial, the worthless seducers, the are gone out from among you, and have withdrawn the inhabitants of their city, the city drawn away, the saying, Let us go and serve other gods, which ye have not known, the enticement to idolatry, the figure, the children of Belial, lawless men, leading a city into apostasy, the truth that wicked men may corrupt a whole community, drawing it to false gods, the figure of the seducers who spread apostasy, the sons of Belial, 'what concord hath Christ with Belial?' 2 Corinthians 6:15, the corruption that spreads from a few lawless men to a whole city, the enticement to unknown gods, the apostasy of the city, vv.12); the children of Belial drawing the city away (the seducers, 2 Corinthians 6:15) — the seducers (children of Belial); 'Let us go and serve other gods, which ye have not known' (the enticement to idolatry, the apostasy spread, the figure, the seducers who corrupt a community, the providence, the seducers, the diligent inquiry into the report to follow, vv.14) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Certain men, the children of Belial, are gone out from among you, and have withdrawn the inhabitants of their city, saying, Let us go and serve other gods, which ye have not known
worthless men drawing the city to serve other gods
the children of Belial leading a whole city into idolatry
the corruption that spreads from a few lawless men to a community (2 Corinthians 6:15)

schools.summary: The diligent inquiry to be made into the report before judgment: 'then shalt thou enquire, and make search, and ask diligently; and, behold, if it be truth, and the thing certain, that such abomination is wrought among you' — before judging, the report was to be carefully investigated (the then shalt thou enquire, and make search, and ask diligently, the threefold diligent inquiry, the and, behold, if it be truth, and the thing certain, the truth established, the that such abomination is wrought among you, the abomination confirmed, the figure, the careful inquiry required before judgment, no rash condemnation, the truth that even in dealing with apostasy, justice required diligent proof, the figure of the care for truth and justice, the report tried before acted on, the threefold inquiry that guards against false accusation, 'the judges shall make diligent inquisition,' Deuteronomy 19:18, the establishing of the fact before the heavy judgment fell, the justice that judges only on certain proof); the diligent threefold inquiry before judgment (the justice, Deuteronomy 19:18) — the inquiry (diligent search); 'if it be truth, and the thing certain, that such abomination is wrought among you' (the fact confirmed before judgment, the certain proof, Deuteronomy 19:18, the figure, justice on certain proof, the providence, the inquiry, the city to be smitten if proved to follow, vv.15) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Then shalt thou enquire, and make search, and ask diligently; and, behold, if it be truth, and the thing certain, that such abomination is wrought among you
the diligent inquiry to be made into the report before judgment
the report carefully investigated before judgment, no rash condemnation
the fact established on diligent proof before the heavy judgment fell (Deuteronomy 19:18)

schools.summary: The apostate city to be utterly destroyed with the sword: 'thou shalt surely smite the inhabitants of that city with the edge of the sword, destroying it utterly, and all that is therein, and the cattle thereof, with the edge of the sword' — the apostate city was to be wholly destroyed (the thou shalt surely smite the inhabitants of that city with the edge of the sword, the city smitten, the destroying it utterly, and all that is therein, the utter destruction, the and the cattle thereof, with the edge of the sword, the cattle too, the figure, the apostate city devoted to destruction, the whole given to the sword, the truth that corporate apostasy brought corporate judgment, the city utterly destroyed, the figure of the herem, the devoted thing wholly destroyed, as the cities of the Canaanites, the gravity of the city's apostasy, the whole given to the sword, vv.16, the judgment that purges idolatry utterly from Israel); the city utterly destroyed for apostasy (the judgment) — the destruction (the city smitten); 'the cattle thereof, with the edge of the sword' (the whole devoted to destruction, the herem, the figure, the apostate city wholly destroyed, the providence, the destruction, the spoil burnt as a whole burnt offering to follow, vv.16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Thou shalt surely smite the inhabitants of that city with the edge of the sword, destroying it utterly, and all that is therein, and the cattle thereof, with the edge of the sword
the apostate city to be utterly destroyed with the sword
the apostate city wholly given to the sword, corporate apostasy bringing corporate judgment
the city devoted to destruction that idolatry be purged from Israel

schools.summary: The spoil burnt and the city made a perpetual heap: 'and thou shalt gather all the spoil of it into the midst of the street thereof, and shalt burn with fire the city, and all the spoil thereof every whit, for the LORD thy God: and it shall be an heap for ever; it shall not be built again' — the city and all its spoil were to be burnt wholly to God, the city never rebuilt (the and thou shalt gather all the spoil of it into the midst of the street thereof, the spoil gathered, the and shalt burn with fire the city, and all the spoil thereof every whit, for the LORD thy God, the whole burnt to God, the and it shall be an heap for ever; it shall not be built again, the perpetual ruin, the figure, the city and spoil wholly burnt to God, a devoted thing, the ruin perpetual, the truth that nothing of the apostate city was to be kept, all given to God in destruction, the figure of the whole burnt offering of judgment, the city consumed to God, the perpetual heap a witness against apostasy, 'it shall not be built again,' the devoted city wholly consumed, the idolatry purged for ever, vv.15); the city and spoil wholly burnt to God (the devoted thing) — the burning (to God); 'it shall be an heap for ever; it shall not be built again' (the city never rebuilt, the perpetual witness, the figure, the ruin against apostasy, the providence, the burning, the blessing on the obedient people to follow, vv.17) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt gather all the spoil of it into the midst of the street thereof, and shalt burn with fire the city, and all the spoil thereof every whit, for the LORD thy God: and it shall be an heap for ever; it shall not be built again
the spoil burnt and the city made a perpetual heap
the city and all its spoil burnt wholly to God, the perpetual heap a witness against apostasy
never to be rebuilt

schools.summary: That nothing of the cursed thing was to be kept, that God might show mercy: 'and there shall cleave nought of the cursed thing to thine hand: that the LORD may turn from the fierceness of his anger, and shew thee mercy, and have compassion upon thee, and multiply thee, as he hath sworn unto thy fathers' — nothing of the devoted city was to be kept, that God's anger be turned and mercy shown (the and there shall cleave nought of the cursed thing to thine hand, nothing of the devoted thing kept, the that the LORD may turn from the fierceness of his anger, the anger turned away, the and shew thee mercy, and have compassion upon thee, and multiply thee, the mercy and increase, the as he hath sworn unto thy fathers, the promise to the fathers, the figure, the devoted thing wholly given up, that God's mercy and blessing follow, the truth that obedience in purging idolatry brings God's compassion and increase, the figure of the blessing that follows the putting away of sin, the mercy and multiplying, the warning of Achan, the cursed thing not to cleave to the hand, Joshua 7:1, the mercy and compassion promised to the obedient, the promise to the fathers fulfilled); nothing of the cursed thing kept, the anger turned (the devoted thing, Joshua 7:1) — the devoted thing (nought kept); 'shew thee mercy, and have compassion upon thee, and multiply thee' (the mercy and increase on obedience, the promise to the fathers, the figure, the blessing that follows purging sin, the providence, the mercy, the blessing on obedience to follow, vv.18) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And there shall cleave nought of the cursed thing to thine hand: that the LORD may turn from the fierceness of his anger, and shew thee mercy, and have compassion upon thee, and multiply thee, as he hath sworn unto thy fathers
that nothing of the cursed thing was to be kept, that God might show mercy
nothing of the devoted city kept, that God's anger be turned and his mercy, compassion, and increase follow
the promise to the fathers fulfilled (Joshua 7:1)

schools.summary: The blessing conditioned on hearkening to God and doing right: 'when thou shalt hearken to the voice of the LORD thy God, to keep all his commandments which I command thee this day, to do that which is right in the eyes of the LORD thy God' — the mercy and blessing were conditioned on obedience to God (the when thou shalt hearken to the voice of the LORD thy God, the hearkening, the to keep all his commandments which I command thee this day, the keeping of all his commands, the to do that which is right in the eyes of the LORD thy God, the doing of right, the figure, the blessing conditioned on full obedience, the hearkening and doing right, the truth that God's mercy and increase follow the keeping of his commandments, the figure of the obedience that draws down blessing, the doing of right in God's eyes, the condition of the promised mercy, full obedience to God's voice, the chapter closed with the call to hearken and do right, vv.17); the blessing conditioned on keeping God's commands (the obedience) — the obedience (hearken and keep); 'to do that which is right in the eyes of the LORD thy God' (the doing of right the condition of blessing, the obedience that blesses, the figure, the obedience that draws down mercy, the providence, the blessing, the laws of holy living to follow, ch.14) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: When thou shalt hearken to the voice of the LORD thy God, to keep all his commandments which I command thee this day, to do that which is right in the eyes of the LORD thy God
the blessing conditioned on hearkening to God and doing right
the mercy and increase conditioned on full obedience
the hearkening and doing right in God's eyes the way of blessing
